# ADR-001 — v6.1 Deep-Review Follow-Ups (R5, R6, R7)

> **Status:** Accepted (v6.1)
> **Audience:** maintainer
> **Context:** Decisions for the deep-review follow-up items whose correct
> deliverable is an explicit, justified decision rather than a mechanical change.
> R1–R4, R8–R16 shipped as code + selftests in v6.1. R5 (split the mode registry
> out of `workflows.yaml`) **also shipped in v6.1** — see below. R6/R7 are the
> remaining design-evaluation decisions (keep the four orchestration agents;
> keep the nine diagnostic agents).

---

## R5 — Split / reduce `workflows.yaml`

**Decision:** **Physically split the mode registry out of `workflows.yaml` —
SHIPPED in v6.1.** The `modes:` block (mode definitions + the `phaseRelevance`
sub-block) was extracted to a dedicated canonical registry,
`bubbles/workflows/modes.yaml`. There is no duplicated copy; `workflows.yaml` no
longer contains a `modes:` block.

**What shipped in v6.1:**
- `workflows.yaml`: **2,737 → 1,400 lines (−1,337, −49%)** — the `modes:`
  monolith is gone; the file now holds only gates, phases, mode templates,
  execution options, and policy blocks.
- `bubbles/workflows/modes.yaml` (1,346 lines): the single canonical mode
  registry. Maintainers edit modes here.
- `mode-resolver.sh` composes `workflows.yaml` + `modes.yaml` at read time —
  byte-identical resolution vs. the pre-split monolith, proven by
  `mode-resolver-selftest.sh` and the 55-mode round-trip in `mode-alias-selftest.sh`.
  Every other mode reader (`state-transition-guard.sh`, `artifact-lint.sh`,
  `delivery-implementation-delta-guard.sh`, `is-terminal-for-mode.sh`,
  `intent-routes-lint.sh`, `generate-framework-stats.sh`, `generate-cheatsheet.sh`,
  `workflow-registry-consistency.sh`, `planning-workflow-chain-guard.sh`,
  `mode-family-inventory.sh`) reads `modes.yaml` directly, with a `workflows.yaml`
  fallback for any pre-split fixture/repo that still carries an inline `modes:` block.
- `bubbles/scripts/generate-modes-block.sh` is a **strip + no-duplication guard**
  (`--strip` removes an inline block; `--check`, wired into `framework-validate`,
  BLOCKS any re-inlining regression).
- `mode-family-inventory.sh` continues to enforce that every mode maps to
  exactly one canonical v6 primitive.

**Further fragmentation into 15 per-family files — decided against (not
deferred):** the review's illustrative mechanism was 15
`bubbles/workflows/<family>.yaml` files. That is unnecessary: extracting the
`modes:` block already eliminates the 2,600-line monolith (R5's actual finding),
per-family inspection/validation is provided by `mode-family-inventory.sh
--family <p>`, and modes are now edited in isolation from the rest of the
config. Splitting a cohesive 1,346-line registry into 15 fragments the resolver
must recompose would add file-sprawl and resolver complexity for no proportional
benefit.

**Revisit trigger:** if `modes.yaml` itself grows past a maintainability
threshold or per-family schema divergence becomes real, reopen and emit
per-family files from the registry — the `mode-family-inventory.sh` grouping
already defines the partition, so the regrouping stays mechanical.

**Consequence:** maintainers MUST keep `mode-family-inventory.sh --check` AND
`generate-modes-block.sh --check` green. A new mode without an `aliases.yaml`
primitive mapping fails framework-validate; re-inlining a `modes:` block into
`workflows.yaml` fails framework-validate.

---

## R6 — Collapse goal / iterate / sprint into `workflow` tags

**Decision:** **Keep the four orchestration agents** (`workflow`, `goal`,
`iterate`, `sprint`) as distinct surfaces. Do **not** collapse them into
`workflow` tags in v6.1.

**Rationale:**
- The four agents share a convergence loop but expose **materially different
  operator contracts**: `goal` (one goal → converge), `iterate` (pick next
  highest-value work), `sprint` (many goals + wall-clock budget), `workflow`
  (explicit mode). These are different *entry intents*, not just tag variations
  — collapsing them would push intent-selection complexity onto the operator at
  the call site, the opposite of the v6 "describe what you want" goal.
- The drift risk that motivated the review note (four agents policed identically
  by `orchestrator-persistence-lint.sh` / G086) is **already mechanically
  contained**: G086 is the shared-contract guard. The agents cannot drift on the
  persistence contract because the lint fails if any of the four deviates.
- Sizes are modest and not duplicative enough to force a merge: `workflow` 657,
  `iterate` 740, `goal` 296, `sprint` 173 lines — the two large ones encode
  genuinely distinct dispatch logic, not copied boilerplate.

**Revisit trigger:** if a fifth orchestrator is proposed, or if G086 has to be
forked per-agent (signalling the contract is no longer truly shared), reopen this
decision and reconsider the tag-collapse.

---

## R7 — Review the 9-agent diagnostic cluster

**Decision:** **Keep the nine diagnostic/certification agents** (`audit`,
`gaps`, `harden`, `test`, `validate`, `regression`, `security`,
`system-review`, `code-review`). Do **not** merge them. Record the explicit
decision and rely on the existing shared-core safeguard.

**Rationale:**
- The nine differ in **what they look for**, which is the right axis to keep
  separate: security threat-modeling, regression baselines, audit verdicts,
  gap closure, and UX/system review are distinct expertises with distinct
  evidence requirements. A merged "diagnostic" agent would either lose that
  specialization or grow into a monolith.
- The shared *contract* ("find findings, route to owner, never edit foreign
  artifacts") is already enforced centrally by gates **G042** (artifact
  ownership), **G056** (validate-only certification), **G061** (rework packets),
  and **G063** (concrete result envelopes), plus shared modules `review-core.md`
  / `audit-core.md` / `validation-core.md`. The cluster cannot drift on the
  contract because the gates are the contract.
- Total size (4,675 lines across 9 files) reflects nine real specializations,
  not redundancy; the largest (`validate` 873, `harden` 708) own unique
  certification logic.

**Safeguard affirmed:** the diagnostic agents share `validation-core.md` and the
route-required envelope contract; no new shared module is required in v6.1.

**Revisit trigger:** if two diagnostic agents begin emitting overlapping finding
sets for the same input (true redundancy), or if a new diagnostic agent would be
a near-duplicate of an existing one, reopen and consider consolidation behind a
shared diagnostic-core.

---

  `bubbles/scripts/generate-modes-block.sh` (`--check` no-duplication guard),
  `bubbles/scripts/mode-resolver.sh` (compose-at-read-time).
